mechanism_name: Incident angle and reflection angle
commonsense_rule: Incident angle ↑ → reflection angle ↑ (the reflected ray moves farther from the normal)
factual_rule: Incident angle ↑ → reflection angle ↑ (the reflected ray moves farther from the normal)
flipped_rule: Incident angle ↑ → reflection angle ↓ (the reflected ray moves closer to the normal)
key_visual_cue: When the incident-ray angle increases, does the reflected-ray angle increase as well?
visual_context: The angles marked by arcs in the figure are the angles between the light rays and the middle dashed reference line, not the angles with the mirror surface itself. The arrows on the yellow straight lines indicate the direction of light propagation.

frame_descriptions.panel_1: The incident ray makes an angle of 10° with the normal, and the incident ray is almost aligned with the normal; the reflected ray makes an angle of 80° with the normal, and the reflected ray is almost horizontally deflected away.
frame_descriptions.panel_2: The incident ray makes an angle of 40° with the normal, and the incident ray is moderately offset from the normal; the reflected ray makes an angle of 50° with the normal, and the reflected ray is moderately offset from the normal.
frame_descriptions.panel_3: The incident ray makes an angle of 80° with the normal, and the incident ray is highly offset from the normal; the reflected ray makes an angle of 10° with the normal, and the reflected ray is almost aligned with the normal.

question: According to the pattern in the image, if a laser pointer beam replaced the drawn incoming ray in the same mirror setup, what would happen when the incoming beam was aimed farther from the dashed reference line?

choices.A: The reflected beam would determine how far from the dashed reference line the incoming beam had been aimed.
choices.B: The reflected beam would also end up farther from the dashed reference line.
choices.C: The reflected beam would end up closer to the dashed reference line.
choices.D: The reflected beam would stay the same.

prior_trap.reason: It matches the usual expectation that reflection angle increases with incident angle.

partial_error.reason: It treats the object substitution as if it erased the visual rule, even though the same induced pattern should still apply.

causal_confusion.reason: It reverses the direction of dependence by making the reflected beam determine the incoming beam.

reasoning_chain: The panels show that as the incident ray moves farther from the dashed reference line, the reflected ray moves closer to that line. That inverse relation is the image-induced rule for this world. Replacing the drawn ray with a laser beam does not change the rule, so aiming the incoming beam farther from the reference line makes the reflected beam end up closer to it.
required_induction: In this world, increasing the incident angle relative to the normal makes the reflected angle decrease.

question: According to the pattern in the image, what would happen if the incident ray's angle from the normal increased to 90 degrees?

choices.A: The reflected ray's angle would determine how large the incident angle becomes.
choices.B: The reflected ray would stay at about 10 degrees from the normal.
choices.C: The reflected ray would also have a larger angle from the normal, bending even farther away from the normal than before.
choices.D: The reflected ray would have an angle from the normal smaller than 10 degrees, so it would be even closer to the normal than in the 80-degree case.

prior_trap.reason: It matches the usual expectation from standard reflection, where a larger incident angle leads to a larger reflection angle.

partial_error.reason: It notices that 10 degrees is the smallest shown reflection angle, but it incorrectly assumes the trend cannot continue beyond the displayed range.

causal_confusion.reason: It reverses the direction of causation by treating the reflected angle as the cause of the incident angle.

reasoning_chain: Across the three panels, the incident angle from the normal increases from 10 degrees to 40 degrees to 80 degrees, while the reflected angle from the normal decreases from 80 degrees to 50 degrees to 10 degrees. This shows the counterfactual rule in this image: larger incident angles produce smaller reflected angles. Therefore, if the incident angle increases further to 90 degrees, the reflected angle should become even smaller than 10 degrees, meaning the reflected ray would sit even closer to the normal.
required_induction: In this world, increasing the incident angle makes the reflected angle smaller relative to the normal.

commonsense_rule: Prism apex angle ↑ → spectral band widens after white-light dispersion (the rainbow band becomes wider)
factual_rule: Prism apex angle ↑ → spectral band widens after white-light dispersion (the rainbow band becomes wider)

key_visual_cue: When the prism apex angle increases, does the spread width of the outgoing rainbow band increase?
visual_context: The rainbow band on the right shows the colored light produced when white light is dispersed by a prism, and the overall spread width of the band is the spectral bandwidth referred to in the figure.

question: According to the pattern in the image, suppose sunlight passes through a prism instead of the white lab beam shown. If the prism's apex angle becomes larger, what will happen to the rainbow band?

choices.A: It will spread out more and become wider.
choices.B: It will keep the same width.
choices.C: The rainbow band's width will determine how large the prism angle is.
choices.D: It will spread out less and become narrower.

prior_trap.reason: It matches ordinary optics intuition that a larger prism angle should create a wider spread of colors.

partial_error.reason: It changes the light source but wrongly assumes the induced angle-to-width rule no longer applies.

causal_confusion.reason: It reverses the causal direction by treating the spectrum width as the cause of the prism angle.

reasoning_chain: The panels show a counterfactual pattern: as the prism's apex angle increases, the rainbow band becomes narrower rather than wider. Because sunlight is still white light, the same induced rule should transfer under this object substitution.
required_induction: In this world, increasing the prism's apex angle makes the dispersed spectrum band narrower.

mechanism_name: Prism refraction and spectral broadening
commonsense_rule: Prism apex angle ↑ → spectral band widens after white-light dispersion (the rainbow band becomes wider)
factual_rule: Prism apex angle ↑ → spectral band widens after white-light dispersion (the rainbow band becomes wider)
flipped_rule: Prism apex angle ↑ → spectral band narrows after white-light dispersion (the rainbow band becomes narrower)
key_visual_cue: When the prism apex angle increases, does the spread width of the outgoing rainbow band increase?
visual_context: The rainbow band on the right shows the colored light produced when white light is dispersed by a prism, and the overall spread width of the band is the spectral bandwidth referred to in the figure.

choices.D: Choose any prism, since the color-band width decides the prism angle.

required_induction: In this world, adding more semitransparent layers makes the transmitted light spot brighter rather than dimmer.

question: According to the pattern in the image, which blocker should you choose if you want the shadow on the ground to cover the most area?

choices.A: Choose the smallest blocker available.
choices.B: Choose the largest blocker available.
choices.C: Choose a medium-sized blocker.
choices.D: Choose any blocker.

prior_trap.reason: It follows the usual expectation that larger opaque objects cast larger shadows.

partial_error.reason: It invents a middle optimum that is not supported by the visual trend.

causal_confusion.reason: It incorrectly flips cause and effect by saying the shadow controls the blocker.

reasoning_chain: The image shows that larger blockers are paired with smaller shadows in this world. Reversing the scene goal, the largest shadow must therefore come from the smallest blocker, not the largest one.
required_induction: In this world, a smaller opaque blocker produces a larger shadow area.
